title: Certifying Nonclassical Proper-Time Histories with a Quantum Clock
abstract: Quantum clocks can acquire relativistic phases from motional or gravitational proper-time differences, but reduced clock dephasing alone does not certify nonclassical proper-time histories. We formulate this distinction as a channel-certification problem. First, we show that any two-level single-time dephasing signal, including one generated by an effective quantum proper-time label, admits a classical random proper-time representation. We then define the convex set of classical mixtures of experimentally specified proper-time histories and prove a Choi-rank separation criterion for conditioned coherent history recombination. A two-branch Ramsey protocol gives explicit bright- and dark-port population witnesses outside this classical set. The certification is operational and relative to the specified history set: it rules out classical mixtures of the same implemented proper-time histories, not arbitrary classical protocols with different histories or controls.
summary: The paper develops a rigorous method to certify nonclassical proper-time histories in quantum clocks by treating the problem as a channel-certification task. It proves that coherent recombination of distinct history paths generates an observable signal that cannot be reproduced by classical mixtures of those same histories. This provides a fundamental, resource-theoretic test for probing relativistic time effects beyond simple dephasing measurements.
main_problem: The core problem is developing a rigorous method to experimentally certify that quantum proper-time histories, imprinted on clock signals by relativistic effects, are genuinely nonclassical and cannot be simulated by classical mixtures of specified history outcomes.
main_result: A Choi-rank separation criterion, demonstrated via a minimal two-branch Ramsey protocol, proves that the conditioned operation associated with coherent recombination of distinct proper-time histories falls outside the convex set of classically simulable channels ($	ext{CPTH}(H)$).
method: The authors employ channel-certification techniques by comparing the measured conditional signal against the theoretical prediction for classical mixtures. They utilize a two-branch Ramsey protocol and develop resource-theoretic diagnostics like the robustness measure $	ext{RH}_{	ext{PTH}}(\Phi)$.
model_or_system: The physical system is based on precision quantum clocks, such as trapped-ion optical clocks, where relativistic time dilation phases are imprinted during clock evolution. The theoretical framework models the process using history unitaries ($V_h$) and analyzes the resulting channel structure.

important_parameters: The effective accumulated phase $	heta_{eff}$ and the fidelity parameters ($\eta_h, \eta_c, \eta_m$) that quantify experimental imperfections.
main_assumptions: The certification is operational and relative only to a finite, specified set of proper-time histories ($H$). The analysis relies on identifying single-Kraus, subnormalized-unitary outcomes for the strongest theoretical bounds.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the comparison between the predicted classical signal ($\langle Z angle_{	ext{cl}} = 0$) and the measured non-zero signals from the Ramsey protocol, demonstrating the separation boundary.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by establishing a hierarchy of classical limitations (single-time dephasing $	o$ averaged histories $	o$ conditioned recombination). It then introduces the Choi-rank criterion using a minimal two-branch witness and concludes with resource-theoretic visibility models to quantify experimental feasibility.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, generalizable quantum resource theory tool—channel certification—applied directly to fundamental physics measurements (time dilation), bridging foundational quantum information concepts with cutting-edge AMO metrology.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Classical mixtures and selective erasure of proper-time histories. (a) Two motional branches |χ1⟩, |χ2⟩ label the specified proper-time histories V± = U±R U±, with U± = e∓iθZ/2. If the motional label is ignored, the clock sees the free mixture Eavg ∈CPTH(H2). (b) Measuring the motion in the history-erasing basis produces the bright port K+ = (V+ + V−)/2, while the orthogonal erasing outcome gives the dark port K−= (V+ −V−)/2. The witness depends on erasing this proper-time-history label.
